topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the rejection email, the crisp white paper feeling suddenly heavy in his hands. He reread the line about the 'superior fit' with a slow, deliberate motion, his jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated across his temples. He had spent six months tailoring his resume, pouring his best energy into that application, only to be sidelined by someone else. When Sarah called, her voice bright and breezy, recounting how she’d secured the position, Liam managed a tight, forced smile that didn't reach his eyes. He excused himself abruptly, the sound of his chair scraping against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet office hallway. He walked out into the afternoon sun, the warmth feeling suddenly abrasive against his skin.

[story 2]

"Of course, it went to her," Mark murmured, the words tasting like ash in his mouth. He leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning under his weight, and slowly picked up his coffee mug, turning it over and over as if searching for some flaw in the ceramic. He felt a distinct heat rising in his chest, a prickly sensation that made his hands tremble slightly. When Clara mentioned her success, her tone was light, almost dismissive, as if sharing a pleasant observation rather than announcing a victory. Mark pushed his chair back with unnecessary force, the sound echoing loudly enough to draw attention. He didn't bother to look at her, just focused intently on the pattern of the carpet, his knuckles white where he gripped the mug.

[story 3]

I watched the notification pop up on my screen. The words swam before my eyes, blurring into meaningless shapes. A slow, cold wave washed over me, starting in my stomach and spreading outward, leaving a hollow, unsettling space. I picked up my phone, my fingers moving mechanically, avoiding the need to respond. When Ben finally cornered me by the water cooler, his expression was one of genuine, unfeigned congratulations. His enthusiasm was so overwhelming, so utterly devoid of any recognition of the effort I’d poured into that application, that I found myself unable to meet his gaze. I just nodded once, a jerky, minimal movement, and turned away, focusing instead on the condensation dripping down the cool metal of the cooler.
